title: Introduction
text: In the previous lesson, we used PageRank to find influential papers in our citation network. Now, we're going to explore a different question: Which papers serve as bridges between different research areas? Betweenness Centrality is the key here. It identifies those connectors—nodes that link different parts of the network together. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s501-000-new] As you can see in this illustration, certain nodes are highlighted to represent Betweenness Centrality. These nodes act as bridges between different clusters, indicating their role in connecting disparate parts of the network.

title: What you'll learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to explain what Betweenness Centrality measures and how it differs from PageRank. You'll also learn how to run Betweenness Centrality using the Python GDS client, compare influence with connectivity using visualization, and identify papers that bridge multiple research areas.

title: What Betweenness measures
text: Betweenness Centrality measures how often a node lies on the shortest paths between other nodes in the network. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s503-000-existing] This conceptual diagram illustrates Betweenness Centrality, showing how a central node lies on multiple shortest paths between other nodes.

title: High Betweenness means high connectivity
text: If a node has high Betweenness, it means many shortest paths pass through it. This makes the node a bottleneck or bridge in the network. If you removed it, many nodes would become harder to reach from each other. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s504-002-new] In this network diagram, notice how a node with high Betweenness is highlighted, illustrating its role as a critical bridge or bottleneck.

title: Betweenness in a social network
text: Think about a social network for a moment. A person with high PageRank is like a celebrity—everyone knows who they are. A person with high Betweenness is like a connector—they introduce people from different social circles to each other. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s505-001-existing] This comparison diagram shows a 'celebrity' node with high PageRank and a 'connector' node with high Betweenness in a social network.

title: Both types matter
text: Both roles are valuable, but for different reasons. The celebrity has influence, while the connector enables information flow across the network. In our citation network, we want to find the papers that play the connector role. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s506-003-new] Here, you can see a side-by-side comparison of a 'celebrity' node with high PageRank and a 'connector' node with high Betweenness, highlighting their distinct roles.

title: How Betweenness is calculated
text: For each node in the graph, the algorithm finds all shortest paths between every pair of nodes, counts how many of those paths pass through the node, and then divides that count by the total number of paths. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s507-002-existing] This step-by-step diagram shows the calculation of Betweenness Centrality, highlighting the process of counting shortest paths through a node.

title: Computational cost
text: Betweenness is more computationally expensive than PageRank because it needs to compute shortest paths between many pairs of nodes. For the Cora dataset with 2,708 nodes, this completes quickly. For larger graphs, you may need to use sampling, which we'll cover later.

title: Setup: Retrieve the projection
text:
First, we retrieve the graph projection we created earlier. Here's how you do it in Python: 

```python
G = gds.graph.get("cora-graph")

print(f"Graph '{G.name()}' loaded:")
print(f"  {G.node_count():} nodes")
print(f"  {G.relationship_count():} relationships")
```

This code snippet will load your graph and print out the number of nodes and relationships.

title: Running Betweenness Centrality
text:
Now we can run Betweenness Centrality and write the results to the database. Here's the Python code for that: 

```python
bc_result = gds.betweenness.write(
    G,
    writeProperty='betweenness'
)

print(f"Computed Betweenness for {bc_result['nodePropertiesWritten']:} papers")
```

This will compute Betweenness for your papers and store the results.

title: Inspecting the distribution
text:
GDS automatically provides distribution statistics in the result object. Here's a quick look at what you get:

| **centralityDistribution** | **computeMillis** | **configuration** | **nodePropertiesWritten** | **postProcessingMillis** | **preProcessingMillis** | **writeMillis** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| {'max': 850,664, 'mean': 6,053, 'min': 0, 'p50': 942, 'p75': 4,729, 'p90': 12,956, 'p95': 23,651, 'p99': 72,896, 'p999': 324,664} | 180 | {'concurrency': 4, 'writeConcurrency': 4, 'writeProperty': 'betweenness', 'nodeLabels': ['*'], 'relationshipTypes': ['*'], 'logProgress': True, 'sudo': False, 'writeToResultStore': False} | 2,708 | 23 | 0 | 9 |

These statistics give you a detailed view of the Betweenness Centrality distribution across your network.

title: Key parameters
text:
Here are the main parameters you can configure when running Betweenness Centrality:

| **Parameter** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| writeProperty | (required) | Property name for storing results |
| samplingSize | all nodes | Number of source nodes for approximation |
| samplingSeed | random | Seed for reproducible sampling |

These parameters allow you to customize the Betweenness calculation to fit your needs.

title: Exact versus approximate
text: By default, Betweenness computes exact results using all nodes as sources. For large graphs, you can use sampling to get approximate results much faster. We'll look at this option later in the lesson. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s513-007-new] This flowchart illustrates the decision-making process for choosing between exact and approximate Betweenness Centrality calculations.

title: Finding bridge papers
text:
With Betweenness scores written to the database, we can query for the top bridge papers. Here's the query:

```python
q_top_betweenness = """
    MATCH (p:Paper)
    WHERE p.betweenness IS NOT NULL
    RETURN
        p.paper_Id AS paperId,
        p.subject AS subject,
        p.betweenness AS betweenness,
        p.pageRank AS pageRank
    ORDER BY p.betweenness DESC
    LIMIT 10
"""

df_bridges = gds.run_cypher(q_top_betweenness)
display(df_bridges)
```

This will return the top 10 papers with the highest Betweenness scores.

title: Interpreting the results
text: Notice how the top papers by Betweenness are often different from the top papers by PageRank. High Betweenness papers act as connectors between research communities. These are often methodological papers whose techniques apply across multiple domains. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s515-004-existing] This image shows the top bridge papers by Betweenness.

title: Interpreting Betweenness scores
text:
Betweenness scores are relative to your specific graph:

* A score of zero means no shortest paths pass through this node (often leaf nodes)
* Low scores indicate regular nodes with few paths passing through them
* High scores indicate bridges or bottlenecks

You should only compare scores within the same graph. A score of 500 means very different things in different networks. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s516-008-new] This diagram categorizes nodes based on their Betweenness scores, showing examples of leaf nodes, regular nodes, and bridge nodes.

title: Comparing PageRank and Betweenness
text: Let's visualize the relationship between influence (PageRank) and connectivity (Betweenness). This helps us understand the different types of important papers in the network.

title: Fetching both metrics
text:
First, we query all papers that have both metrics computed. Here's the query:

```python
q_both_metrics = """
    MATCH (p:Paper)
    WHERE p.pageRank IS NOT NULL
      AND p.betweenness IS NOT NULL
    RETURN p.pageRank AS pageRank,
           p.betweenness AS betweenness,
           p.subject AS subject
"""

df_metrics = gds.run_cypher(q_both_metrics)
```

This will fetch all papers with both PageRank and Betweenness scores.

title: Creating a scatter plot
text:
Now we can create a scatter plot to see how the two metrics relate. Here's how you do it:

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.scatter(df_metrics['pageRank'],
            df_metrics['betweenness'],
            alpha=0.5, s=10)
plt.xlabel('PageRank (Influence)')
plt.ylabel('Betweenness Centrality (Connectivity)')
plt.title('PageRank vs Betweenness in Citation Network')
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
```

This scatter plot will help you visualize the relationship between PageRank and Betweenness.

title: The scatter plot reveals four quadrants
text: [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s520-006-existing] This scatter plot of PageRank vs Betweenness reveals four distinct quadrants.

title: Bottom-left: Regular papers
text: Most papers cluster in the bottom-left quadrant. These have low influence and low connectivity. This is expected—the majority of papers in any network are not exceptional by either measure.

title: Top-right: Superstar papers
text: Papers in the top-right quadrant are both influential AND highly connected. These are rare papers that are widely cited and also serve as bridges between research areas.

title: Top-left: Bridge papers
text: Papers in the top-left quadrant have high connectivity but moderate influence -- none here. These are true bridge papers—they connect different research areas even though they may not be the most famous works.

title: Bottom-right: Influential specialists
text: Papers in the bottom-right quadrant are influential but not strongly connecting different areas. These are important papers within their specific field, but they don't bridge to other research communities.

title: Combining algorithms reveals richer insights
text: Different algorithms reveal different types of importance in your network. Using PageRank alone, you would miss the bridge papers. Using Betweenness alone, you would miss the influential specialists. Combining them gives you a more complete picture.

title: Cross-subject bridge analysis
text:
Let's find out which research subjects produce the most bridge papers. Here's the query:

```python
q_subject_bridges = """
    MATCH (p:Paper)
    WHERE p.betweenness > 1000
    RETURN
        p.subject AS subject,
        count(p) AS num_bridge_papers,
        avg(p.betweenness) AS avg_betweenness,
        avg(p.pageRank) AS avg_pageRank
    ORDER BY num_bridge_papers DESC
"""

df_subject_bridges = gds.run_cypher(q_subject_bridges)
display(df_subject_bridges)
```

This will analyze bridge papers by subject.

title: Interpreting subject analysis
text:
Subjects with many high-Betweenness papers tend to be methodological fields that influence multiple domains. These are the research areas where techniques and ideas flow outward to other communities.

| **subject** | **num_bridge_papers** | **avg_betweenness** | **avg_pageRank** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Neural_Networks | 401 | 11,854 | 1.24 |
| Theory | 206 | 12,227 | 1.37 |
| Probabilistic_Methods | 205 | 13,763 | 1.31 |
| Genetic_Algorithms | 188 | 14,365 | 1.45 |
| Case_Based | 159 | 9,407 | 1.24 |
| Reinforcement_Learning | 108 | 13,188 | 1.44 |
| Rule_Learning | 73 | 6,838 | 1.22 |

This table shows the bridge papers by research subject.

title: Finding specific cross-subject bridges
text: We can also find papers that specifically cite across multiple different research areas. These are the truly interdisciplinary works.

title: Querying cross-subject bridges
text:
This query finds papers with high Betweenness that cite at least three different subjects:

```python
q_cross_subject = """
    MATCH (p:Paper)-[:CITES]->(cited:Paper)
    WHERE p.betweenness > 500
      AND p.subject <> cited.subject
    WITH p,
         collect(DISTINCT cited.subject) AS cited_subjects,
         count(DISTINCT cited.subject) AS num_subjects_cited
    WHERE num_subjects_cited >= 3
    RETURN
        p.paper_Id AS paperId,
        p.subject AS subject,
        p.betweenness AS betweenness,
        p.pageRank AS pageRank,
        num_subjects_cited,
        cited_subjects
    ORDER BY num_subjects_cited DESC, p.betweenness DESC
    LIMIT 10
"""

df_cross_subject = gds.run_cypher(q_cross_subject)
pp(df_cross_subject)
```

This will find papers that bridge multiple research areas.

title: Truly interdisciplinary papers
text:
These papers have both structural importance (high Betweenness) and actually cite work from three or more different fields. They serve as knowledge transfer hubs, synthesizing ideas from across the research landscape.

| **paperId** | **subject** | **betweenness** | **pageRank** | **num_subjects_cited** | **cited_subjects** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 28254 | Neural_Networks | 8,692 | 1.75 | 4 | Case_Based, Reinforcement_Learning, Theory, Probabilistic_Methods |
| 299197 | Neural_Networks | 82,818 | 1.08 | 3 | Genetic_Algorithms, Reinforcement_Learning, Probabilistic_Methods |
| 35797 | Neural_Networks | 78,154 | 2.90 | 3 | Probabilistic_Methods, Theory, Rule_Learning |
| 46431 | Neural_Networks | 30,303 | 0.95 | 3 | Case_Based, Theory, Genetic_Algorithms |
| 1104449 | Neural_Networks | 18,376 | 0.81 | 3 | Theory, Case_Based, Probabilistic_Methods |
| 97892 | Case_Based | 17,265 | 0.83 | 3 | Reinforcement_Learning, Theory, Rule_Learning |
| 14549 | Theory | 10,824 | 1.30 | 3 | Neural_Networks, Reinforcement_Learning, Probabilistic_Methods |
| 1103676 | Neural_Networks | 5,063 | 0.68 | 3 | Case_Based, Probabilistic_Methods, Theory |
| 323128 | Theory | 4,610 | 0.86 | 3 | Probabilistic_Methods, Case_Based, Neural_Networks |
| 68463 | Rule_Learning | 1,673 | 0.67 | 3 | Case_Based, Theory, Genetic_Algorithms |

This table lists papers bridging multiple research areas.

title: Sampling for large graphs
text: For large graphs with millions of nodes, exact Betweenness computation can be slow. You can use sampling to get approximate results much faster. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s531-012-new] This flowchart explains the process and benefits of using sampling for Betweenness Centrality in large graphs.

title: Using the samplingSize parameter
text:
The `samplingSize` parameter limits how many source nodes are used in the calculation. Here's how you can run Betweenness with sampling:

```python
bc_sampled_result = gds.betweenness.write(
    G,
    writeProperty='betweenness_sampled',
    samplingSize=500,
    samplingSeed=42
)

print(f"Computed sampled Betweenness for {bc_sampled_result['nodePropertiesWritten']:} papers")
```

This code will compute sampled Betweenness for your papers.

title: Comparing exact and sampled results
text:
You can compare exact and sampled results to see how close the approximation is. Here's the query:

```python
q_compare = """
    MATCH (p:Paper)
    WHERE p.betweenness IS NOT NULL
      AND p.betweenness_sampled IS NOT NULL
    RETURN p.paper_Id AS paperId,
           p.betweenness AS exact,
           p.betweenness_sampled AS sampled
    ORDER BY p.betweenness DESC
    LIMIT 10
"""

df_compare = gds.run_cypher(q_compare)
display(df_compare)
```

This will compare the exact and sampled Betweenness scores.

title: When to use sampling
text:
Consider using sampling when:

* Your graph has millions of nodes
* Exact computation takes too long for your use case
* Approximate results are acceptable for your analysis

For the Cora dataset with 2,708 nodes, exact computation is fast enough that sampling isn't necessary. On larger graphs, sampling can significantly reduce computation time while still identifying the most important bridge nodes.

title: Performance considerations
text:
Betweenness is more expensive than PageRank for a few reasons:

* It computes shortest paths between many node pairs
* Memory usage increases with the level of parallelism
* Undirected graphs are more expensive than directed graphs

[IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s536-015-new] This diagram illustrates the factors contributing to the computational expense of Betweenness Centrality compared to PageRank.

title: Handling memory issues
text:
If you run into memory problems on large graphs, you can:

* Reduce the `concurrency` parameter to use fewer parallel threads
* Use `samplingSize` to compute approximate results
* Consider whether you need exact Betweenness or if another centrality measure would suffice

[IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s537-016-new] This flowchart or decision tree helps you handle memory issues when computing Betweenness Centrality on large graphs.

title: Common pitfall: Disconnected graphs
text: Betweenness only counts paths that actually exist in the graph. If your graph has isolated components, nodes may have zero Betweenness even if they are central within their own component. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s538-005-existing] This illustration shows a disconnected graph with isolated components, highlighting how Betweenness Centrality is affected.

title: Common pitfall: Comparing across graphs
text: A Betweenness score of 5000 means nothing without context. You should only compare scores within the same graph. The same score in a different network would have a completely different meaning. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s539-017-new] This illustration emphasizes the importance of context when interpreting Betweenness scores, highlighting intra-graph comparisons.

title: Common pitfall: Expecting similarity to PageRank
text: High PageRank does not imply high Betweenness, and vice versa. They measure fundamentally different things: Influence versus connectivity. The fact that they identify different papers is a feature, not a bug. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-7-betweenness-deep-dive-s540-018-new] This comparison chart or diagram illustrates the differences between PageRank and Betweenness Centrality, highlighting their distinct purposes.

title: Summary
text:
Betweenness Centrality identifies bridges and bottlenecks in your network:

* It measures how often a node lies on shortest paths between other nodes
* High-Betweenness nodes are connectors that link different parts of the network
* It complements PageRank by revealing a different type of importance: Connectivity rather than influence
* For large graphs, use the `samplingSize` parameter to get approximate results faster
* Always compare scores within the same graph, never across different networks

You've now used both PageRank and Betweenness to analyze the citation network, finding both influential papers and bridge papers. Next, we'll use Louvain community detection to discover research clusters in the network.
